revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ishibutai-Kofun]][[en:Ishibutai Kofun]][[fr:Kofun d'Ishibutai]][[it:Kofun Ishibutai]][[ja:石舞台古墳]][[zh:石舞台古墳]]
{{Short description|Stone megalithic tumulus near Nara, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |name = Ishibutai Kofun |native_name = 石舞台古墳 {{in lang|ja}} |alternate_name = Ishibutoya Kofun |image = File:Ishibutai-kofun Asuka Nara pref05n4592.jpg |alt = |caption = Ishibutai Kofun |map_type = Japan |map_alt = |map_size = |coordinates = {{Coord|34.41678889|N|135.82615|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |location = {{ill|Asuka|qid=Q752397|1=Asuka, Nara}} |region = {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai|1=Kansai region}} |type = Tomb | part_of = |length = |width = |area = {{Convert|27|sqm|sqft|0|abbr=on}} |height = |builder = |material = |built = |abandoned = |epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = |ownership = |management = |public_access = |website = |notes = |commemorates = {{ill|Soga no Umako|qid=Q1191233|1=Soga no Umako}} }}
{{Nihongo|'''Ishibutai kofun'''|石舞台古墳}} is a stone ''[[kofun]]'' or {{ill|Tumulus|en|Tumulus|qid=Q34023|lt=Tumulus|1=Tumulus}} of the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} in the east of Shimanoshō, {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|qid=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The kofun is believed to be the tomb of [[Soga no Umako]]. It occupies an area of {{Convert|27|sqm|sqft|0|abbr=on}}, and is the largest known megalithic structure in Japan.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) "Large Encyclopedia of Japan (Nipponika)") |title=Ishibutai Kofuni |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-01-11 |year=2011 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The kofun is also known as the {{nihongo|Ishibutoya|石太屋}} Kofun.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 "Compendium of Japanese Historical Place names") |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2011-12-23 |year=2011 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> It is located 5 km from [[Tanzan Shrine]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Google Maps |url=https://www.google.com/maps/dir/'34.466861,135.826139'/34.465833,135.861667/@34.464802,135.8394253,15.5z/data=!4m7!4m6!1m3!2m2!1d135.826139!2d34.466861!1m0!3e2?hl=en&entry=ttu |access-date=2023-10-26 |website=Google Maps |language=en}}</ref>
== name == The name of the ''kofun'' in Japanese is a combination of two words, the first, {{nihongo|''ishi''|石}}, meaning "{{ill|Stone|en|Stone|qid=Q22731|lt=Stone|1=Stone}}", and the second, {{nihongo|''butai''|舞台}}, meaning "[[:en:stage (theater)|stage]]". The name of the ''kofun'' therefore originates in its resemblance to a large stone stage.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen (デジタル大辞泉 "Digital Dictionary) |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-01-10 |year=2012 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The Ishibutai ''kofun'' has been known by this name at least as early as the [[:en:Tokugawa period|Tokugawa period]], as evidenced by its entry in the ''Saigoku sanjūsansho meisho zue'', a large guide to Buddhist [[:en:Buddhist pilgrimage|pilgrimage sites]] written by [[Kanenari Akatsuki]] in 1853.<ref>{{cite book | last = Akatsuki | first = Kanenari | title = Saigoku sanjūsansho meisho zue | publisher = Rinsenshoten | location = Kyōto | year = 1991 | isbn = 978-4-653-02200-8 }}</ref>
== Association with Soga no Umako == The Ishibutai Kofun is inferred to be the tomb of [[Soga no Umako]] (559? - 626), and his death during the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} is recorded in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. :Summer, 5th month, 20th day. The Oho-omi died. He was buried in the tomb at Momohama.<ref> {{cite book |last = Lastname |first = Firstname |title = Nihongi; chronicles of Japan from the earliest times to A.D. 697 |publisher = C.E. Tuttle Co |location = Rutland, Vt |year = 1972 |isbn = 0-8048-0984-4 |pages= 154}}</ref>
The historian and archeologist [[Sadakichi Kita]] (1871 - 1939) proposed that the Ishibutai Kofun is the above-mentioned "Momohana" tomb in the ''Nihon Shoki''. Kita also proposed that the earthen mound of the Ishibutai Kofun was removed after Soga no Umako's death as a punishment of the Soga clan by the imperial government.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 "Compendium of Japanese Historical Place names") |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2011-12-23 |year=2011 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Structure == [[File:Ishibutai-kofun Asuka Nara pref06n4592.jpg|thumb|left|Stone entrance of the Ishibutai Kofun]] The Ishibutai Kofun was built on a [[:en:Spur (mountain)|spur]] of a mountain that falls northeast to a small {{ill|Plateau|en|Plateau|qid=Q75520|lt=Plateau|1=Plateau}}. It consists of a platform, a {{ill|Long gallery|en|Long gallery|qid=Q164419|lt=gallery|1=Long gallery}} entryway, stone walls that form a tomb, two large stones that form a ceiling for the tomb, and s on either side of the tomb. In total 30 stones were used to construct the Ishibutai Kofun. The large granite megaliths come from [[Mount Tōnomine]], which is approximately {{convert|3|km|mi|sp=us}} from the site.
=== Platform and moat === The Ishibutai ''kofun'' originally occupied a much larger area than is evidenced by the existing stone tumulus. It was built on a square platform, which measured {{Convert|50|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} on each side at the time of construction. Archeological excavations have revealed that the ''kofun'' was surrounded by a {{ill|Moat|en|Moat|qid=Q88480|lt=Moat|1=Moat}}, a feature typical of other ''kofun'' of the period. This moat is estimated to be {{Convert|12|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} wide. In total the ''kofun'', platform, and moat probably covered an area {{Convert|85|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} long.
=== Approach === ''Kofun'' typically had a stone approach to the entrance path of the tomb. The Ishibutai has a particularly long entrance path, which measures {{Convert|38|m|ft|0|abbr=on}}. A shallow drainage channel {{Convert|11.5|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} long and {{Convert|2.55|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} wide runs the length of the entrance path.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 "Compendium of Japanese Historical Place names") |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2011-12-23 |year=2011 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> This entrance was originally covered like the tomb, but its stone ceiling no longer exists.
=== Tomb === The Ishibutai ''kofun'' is a {{nihongo|''yokoana''横穴墓|lt=Yokoanabo}} {{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type tomb|ja|横穴墓|1=Yokoanabo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-type kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoanabo|qq=}}. The inner chamber is {{Convert|7.5|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} long, {{Convert|3.4|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} wide, and {{Convert|4.8|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} high. It consists of 30 stones, {{Convert|4.7|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} by {{Convert|3.5|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} and {{Convert|7.7|m|ft|0|abbr=on}}. Small {{ill|drainage|en|drainage|qid=Q7481320|lt=drainage|1=Drainage}} channels run along the east, north, and west of the tomb. These were constructed to drain water collects on the north side of the tomb to feed south into the shallow drainage channel in the kofun entry.
=== Ceiling === The Ishibutai ''kofun'' is especially noted for the megaliths that form the ceiling of the tomb. The ceiling of the tomb is formed by two megaliths, one to the north and one to the south. The megalith at the north of the tomb weighs approximately {{convert|60|t|LT|0|abbr=on}}, and the larger stone to the south weighs approximately {{convert|77|t|LT|0|abbr=on}}.
=== Mound === The Ishibutai ''kofun'' was originally a large, flat {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}} type ''kofun''; the existing stone structure was covered at the time of construction by a broad, flat earthen mound. This mound eroded slowly after the construction of the kofun, thus exposing the large megaliths of the tomb roof.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten (国史大辞典 "Dictionary of Japanese History") |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-01-10 |year=2012 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Excavation == [[File:IshibutaiWithFigureSmallVersion2.jpg|right|thumb|250px|Ishibutai Kofun]] The Ishibutai Kofun was first [[:en:Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] by the archaeologist and academic {{ill|Kōsaku Hamada|en|Kōsaku Hamada|qid=Q366007|lt=Kōsaku Hamada|1=Kōsaku Hamada}} (1881－1938).<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-12-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> Imperial kofun have not been readily excavated in Japan. Due to its association with Soga no Umako, the Ishibutai tumulus does not have an imperial designation, and has thus seen extensive excavation.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) "Large Encyclopedia of Japan (Nipponika)") |title=Ishibutai Kofuni |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-01-11 |year=2011 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The kofun was first excavated in 1933, work on the base and moat began in 1935, and excavation of the tomb continued until 1975.
The Ishibutai Kofun excavation yielded no significant finds. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|qid=Q1399692|lt=Funerary objects|1=Grave goods}} were probably lost to {{ill|Grave robbery|en|Grave robbery|qid=Q1537376|lt=Grave robbery|1=Grave robbery}} quite soon after its construction. Stone {{ill|Sherd|en|Sherd|qid=Q1463658|lt=shards|1=Sherd}} to the southeast of the tomb are the remains of a {{ill|Tuff|en|Tuff|qid=Q484924|lt=Tuff|1=Tuff}} {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|qid=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus|1=Sarcophagus}}. Numerous examples of {{ill|Gilding|en|Gilding|qid=Q1334300|lt=gilt|1=Gilding}} and {{ill|Bronze|en|Bronze|qid=Q34095|lt=Bronze|1=Bronze}} implements, as well as {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|qid=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware|1=Earthenware}} shards were found in the banks of the tomb approach. The excavation also revealed that other similar flat stone kofun were built to the north and south of the existing structure.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei (日本歴史地名大系 "Compendium of Japanese Historical Place names") |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2011-12-23 |year=2011 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
The Ishibutai Kofun was designated a historical remain in 1935. In 1954 the kofun was fully designated as a {{nihongo|[[Monuments of Japan|Special Historic Sites]]|特別史跡|tokubetsu shiseki}},<ref>{{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia=Nihon Kokugo Daijiten (日本国語大辞典 "Large Dictionary of the Japanese Language") |title=Ishibutai Kofun |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-01-10 |year=2012 |publisher=Netto Adobansusha |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> one of only 75 sites in Japan with this designation. As excavation of the Ishibutai Kofun continued after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, significant reconstruction of areas around the kofun were carried out. The kofun and its surrounding area is part of the {{ill|Asuka Historical National Government Park|en|Asuka Historical National Government Park|qid=Q4811855|lt=Asuka Historical National Government Park|1=Asuka Historical National Government Park}}.
== Transportation == The Ishibutai Kofun is accessible from {{ill|Asuka Station|en|Asuka Station|qid=Q4811860|lt=Asuka Station|1=Asuka Station}}, which is served by the {{ill|Kintetsu Railway|en|Kintetsu Railway|qid=Q1531085|lt=Kintetsu|1=Kintetsu Railway}} {{ill|Yoshino Line|en|Yoshino Line|qid=Q901085|lt=Yoshino Line|1=Yoshino Line}}. The "Kame Bus" (Tortoise Bus), also called the Asuka Tour Bus/Asuka Circle Route Bus, serves all locations within the {{ill|Asuka Historical National Government Park|en|Asuka Historical National Government Park|qid=Q4811855|lt=Asuka Historical National Government Park|1=Asuka Historical National Government Park}}, and leaves hourly from Asuka Station. {{ill|Bicycle|en|Bicycle|qid=Q11442|lt=Bicycle|1=Bicycle}} rentals are also available at numerous points around the station, and cost approximately 1000 yen a day.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.asuka-park.go.jp/asuka_en/access/ennai.html |title=Movement around the Asuka Area |access-date=2012-01-13 |archive-date=2013-01-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130105111913/http://www.asuka-park.go.jp/asuka_en/access/ennai.html |url-status=dead }}</ref>
== Gallery == <gallery> File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|Stairs to Ishibutai Kofun File:Ishibutai illuminated.jpg|Ishibutai Kofun illuminated in autumn File:Ishibutai-kofun Asuka Nara pref08s4592.jpg|Ceiling of Ishibutai Kofun File:Ishibutai-kofun Asuka Nara pref09n4592.jpg|Park surrounding Ishibutai Kofun </gallery>
==See also==
*[[:en:List of megalithic sites|List of megalithic sites]] *{{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}}
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20130105112411/http://www.asuka-park.go.jp/asuka_en/tumulus/index.html Kofun] * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html 飛鳥資料館・石舞台古墳]{{in lang|ja}} * [http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/ASUKA4/soga/soga09_3.html 飛鳥資料館・馬子の墓]{{in lang|ja}}
